chapter: Chapter 2: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

questions.question: Why was the invention of the microscope important for biology?
questions.answer: It allowed scientists to observe cells and microorganisms.

questions.question: What discovery did Robert Hooke make using cork?
Why are cells considered the structural unit of life?
questions.answer: All organisms are made up of cells.

questions.question: What property of the cell membrane controls material movement?
Which cell component contains genetic material?
What type of organism performs all life functions within one cell?
Which microorganism is responsible for fermentation in bread making?
What chemical change occurs when milk turns into curd?
Why are decomposer microorganisms essential in ecosystems?
questions.answer: They recycle nutrients from dead organisms.

questions.question: Which bacteria improve soil fertility through nitrogen fixation?
Where do Rhizobium bacteria live?
Why are microalgae important for oxygen production?
What ecological role do microorganisms play in nutrient cycles?
questions.answer: They decompose organic matter and recycle nutrients.

questions.question: What structure provides rigidity to plant cells?
What is the functional unit of life?
What group of microorganisms includes yeast?
Which microorganism is commonly found in pond water?
What biological process converts sugars into alcohol or acids?
Why is compost beneficial for soil?
Which process converts atmospheric nitrogen into plant nutrients?
Why are microorganisms vital for agriculture?
questions.answer: They improve soil fertility and nutrient availability.

questions.question: Which part of the cell contains organelles responsible for metabolism?
What is microbiology?
Why are microorganisms important for ecosystem balance?
questions.answer: They recycle nutrients and support food chains.

questions.question: What is the main reason microorganisms are called the invisible living world?
questions.answer: They are living organisms that are too small to be seen without magnification.
